title: What Goes Into a Context Window
concept: System instructions, retrieved documents, tool outputs, conversation history, and scratch memory as distinct layers.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: context engineering means curating EVERYTHING in the context window, not just the...

options.text: model's training data from years ago

exercises.explain: Context engineering goes beyond writing a clever prompt — it's about deciding everything the model sees: documents, history, tool results, and more.

cards.title: One Window, Many Puzzle Pieces
cards.body: Ever wonder what an AI actually 'sees' before it answers you? It's not just your question — it's a stack of different puzzle pieces all loaded into the same window at once.

cards.title: Five Layers of a Context Window
cards.body: A context window is often built from distinct layers: system instructions (the rules), retrieved documents (facts pulled in), tool outputs (results from actions like search or calculators), conversation history (what's been said so far), and scratch memory (notes the AI keeps for itself). Each layer feeds different information into the same window.

cards.title: Think of a Student's Backpack
cards.body: A good student packs their backpack with different things for different reasons: a rulebook (system instructions), library books (retrieved documents), a calculator's printout (tool outputs), notes from today's class discussion (conversation history), and a scratch pad for jotting reminders (scratch memory). Each item plays a different role, even though they all sit in the same bag.

cards.title: A Customer Support Bot in Action
cards.body: System instructions say 'Be polite and only answer about our product.' Retrieved documents pull in the refund policy page. A tool output shows the user's order status from the database. Conversation history remembers the user already said their order number. Scratch memory jots down 'user is frustrated, be extra careful.' All five layers combine into one answer.

cards.title: Myth: It's All Just 'The Prompt'
cards.body: Beginners often think everything typed to an AI is one blob called 'the prompt.' In reality, engineers separate it into layers on purpose — mixing them up carelessly can confuse the model or leak the wrong info.

cards.title: Static Rules vs. Live Memory
cards.body: Some layers barely change during a conversation, while others update constantly as things happen.

left.text: Fixed rules set once, rarely change mid-chat — like a constitution.

right.text: Grows and shifts with every new message — like a flowing river.

exercises.prompt: Which layer would contain the actual numbers returned by a calculator function the AI just called?
exercises.explain: Tool outputs are results that come back after the AI uses a tool, like a calculator, search engine, or database. That's exactly where calculator results would go.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Conversation history and scratch memory are the exact same thing.
exercises.explain: False! Conversation history is the record of what was actually said back and forth. Scratch memory is more like private notes the AI keeps for itself, which may not even be shown to the user.

exercises.sentence: Facts pulled in from a company's knowledge base to help answer a question are called retrieved documents.
exercises.explain: When an AI system pulls in outside facts or files to ground its answer, those are called retrieved documents — a distinct layer from the AI's own rules or memory.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three context window layers was most likely generated by an AI system itself, rather than provided by a human or an external source?

options.text: A support policy PDF uploaded by the company
A short note the AI wrote to itself: 'User already confirmed their email, no need to ask again.'
The user's typed question about their order

exercises.explain: That's scratch memory — a note the AI generates and keeps for its own use, unlike documents uploaded by humans or messages typed by users.

exercises.prompt: A coding assistant is helping a developer debug an error. It has: (1) rules saying 'always suggest safe code,' (2) a Stack Overflow snippet it pulled in, (3) the result of running the code just now, (4) the last 10 messages of chat, and (5) a private note saying 'user tried this fix already, don't repeat it.' Which layer is item (5)?
exercises.explain: Item (5) is scratch memory: a private, self-generated note the AI keeps to track useful context (like 'don't repeat this fix') that isn't part of the official chat, the rules, or any pulled-in document. Recognizing these five distinct layers — system instructions, retrieved documents, tool outputs, conversation history, and scratch memory — is the foundation of context engineering: each layer needs its own careful curation to build a context window that actually helps the model succeed.
